phase_detection.instructions: (!!document.querySelector('.jspsych-instructions-nav') || !!document.querySelector('#jspsych-fullscreen-btn') || /Welcome to the experiment|Your main task is to respond to white arrows|It is really important that you do not wait for the stop signal|switch to full screen mode/i.test(document.body.innerText))
phase_detection.feedback: /correct response|incorrect response|too slow|too fast|correct stop|remember: try to stop|Average response time|Proportion missed go|Proportion correct stops|next block starts in|GO TRIALS:|STOP-SIGNAL TRIALS:/i.test(document.body.innerText)

timing.response_window_js: (() => { const e = document.querySelector('img#jspsych-image-keyboard-response-stimulus'); return !!e && (e.src.includes('go_left') || e.src.includes('go_right') || e.src.includes('stop_left') || e.src.includes('stop_right')); })()
timing.trial_context_js: (() => { try { return 'block:' + (typeof block_ind !== 'undefined' ? block_ind : 'na') + ' trial:' + (typeof trial_ind !== 'undefined' ? trial_ind : 'na') + ' SSD:' + (typeof SSD !== 'undefined' ? SSD : 'na'); } catch (e) { return ''; } })()

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GPH, Beste C, Bissett PG, Brockett AT, Brown JW, Chamberlain SR, Chambers CD, Colonius H, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean go RT in young adults typically ranges from approximately 450 to 600 ms in standard versions of the stop-signal task.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean correct go RT was 539 ms (SD = 86) in a sample of 60 undergraduates running the standard STOP-IT protocol.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Estimated mu values for choice-RT data sets clustered between roughly 380 and 470 ms across experimental conditions.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Across published choice-RT datasets, sigma estimates ranged from approximately 20 to 60 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote A, Popiel SJ, Mewhort DJK
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma parameters for two-choice RT distributions in the Stroop task were on the order of 30-50 ms.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau, the exponential tail parameter, varied from roughly 60 to 160 ms across reviewed datasets and was the most condition-sensitive ex-Gaussian parameter.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Dolan CV, Logan GD, Brown SD, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Posterior mean estimates of tau for the go process were in the range of approximately 70 to 130 ms across participants.

go_left.rationale: Healthy young-adult go RT on STOP-IT averages ~520 ms with characteristic positive skew. Ex-Gaussian decomposition for choice-RT in stop-signal paradigms typically yields mu ~400-450 ms (modal Gaussian component), sigma ~30-50 ms (Gaussian spread reflecting motor/perceptual noise), and tau ~80-120 ms (exponential tail capturing attentional lapses and slow trials). Mean = mu + tau ≈ 520 ms aligns with Verbruggen & Logan benchmarks.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Left and right go responses produced equivalent mean RTs in the STOP-IT validation sample.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GPH, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: No systematic lateral asymmetry between left- and right-hand go responses is observed in standard two-choice stop-signal data.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for choice-RT distributions ranged from approximately 20 to 60 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Dolan CV, Logan GD, Brown SD, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Tau estimates for the go-RT distribution were on the order of 70-130 ms across participants.

go_right.rationale: Right-hand and left-hand go responses are matched in this two-alternative forced choice; no systematic lateral asymmetry is reported in standard STOP-IT data, so mu/sigma/tau mirror go_left.

citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Under the independent race model, signal-respond RTs are systematically faster than no-signal RTs because only the faster portion of the go RT distribution escapes inhibition.

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean signal-respond RT was approximately 50-80 ms shorter than mean no-signal go RT, consistent with the prediction of the independent race model.

citations.authors: Band GPH, van der Molen MW, Logan GD
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: The signal-respond RT distribution is a left-truncated subset of the go RT distribution; its variance is therefore reduced relative to the unconstrained go distribution.

citations.authors: Band GPH, van der Molen MW, Logan GD
citations.title: Horse-race model simulations of the stop-signal procedure
citations.quote: Slow trials are preferentially inhibited, which truncates the upper tail of the signal-respond RT distribution and reduces tau relative to the no-signal go distribution.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Dolan CV, Logan GD, Brown SD, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: The exponential tail of the signal-respond RT distribution was attenuated relative to the no-signal go distribution, reflecting selective inhibition of slow go trials.

stop_signal.rationale: Per the independent race model, failed-inhibit (signal-respond) RTs are systematically faster than go RTs because only the faster tail of the go-process distribution wins the race against the stop-process. Empirical signal-respond means run ~50-80 ms below go means; mu shifts down ~40 ms, tau is truncated (slow trials are selectively inhibited), and sigma narrows slightly. Mean ≈ 460 ms.

citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Reaction times on trials immediately following an error were systematically slower than baseline, by tens of milliseconds.

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HLJ, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes reported across the reviewed studies were typically in the 10-50 ms range.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go RT was approximately 25-35 ms slower on trials following failed-stop (signal-respond) errors than on baseline go-after-go trials.

citations.authors: Dutilh G, van Ravenzwaaij D, Nieuwenhuis S, van der Maas HLJ, Forstmann BU, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing dissipated rapidly, with most of the effect concentrated in the first 1-3 trials following the error.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Slowing was observed exclusively on subsequent go trials; the comparison was undefined for stop trials, on which RT is not the dependent measure.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Both failed-stop (signal-respond) errors and choice errors on go trials produced subsequent RT slowing.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing is robustly documented in stop-signal paradigms, particularly after failed-inhibit trials (signal-respond errors). Effect magnitude is ~20-40 ms on the trial immediately following the error, decaying over ~2-3 subsequent trials. Reflects strategic adjustment of response threshold, not just conflict-driven inhibition.

citations.title: Sustaining attention to simple tasks: A meta-analytic review of the neural mechanisms of vigilant attention
citations.quote: RTs in sustained-attention tasks typically show a gradual increase of the order of 10-20 ms per block over a 30-60 minute task.

citations.title: Sustaining attention to simple tasks: A meta-analytic review of the neural mechanisms of vigilant attention
citations.quote: Accuracy decrements over time-on-task in simple choice tasks are typically small, on the order of 0.1-0.5 percentage points per block.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Aron AR, Band GPH, et al.
citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Time-on-task effects influence both go and stop performance; SSRT can drift modestly across blocks alongside go RT.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Time-on-task effects in stop-signal tasks are modest given the short blocks (64 trials) and 15 s breaks. Go RTs typically increase ~10-15 ms per block across the 4 experimental blocks, with a small parallel rise in go errors. Stop-signal SSRT is relatively stable but signal-respond RT tracks go RT drift.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: RTs decrease as a power function of practice, with the largest decrements occurring early in training.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: A 32-trial practice block precedes the experimental blocks to allow participants to stabilize the stimulus-response mapping.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice-related RT improvements follow a negatively accelerated curve, asymptoting after several dozen trials in simple choice tasks.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Practice effects are observed primarily on the speeded go responses; SSRT estimates stabilize quickly given the SSD tracking procedure.

practice_effect.rationale: Small early-task speedup (~10-20 ms) over the first block as participants stabilize stimulus-response mappings. Largely absorbed by the 32-trial practice block but residual learning continues into block 1. Negligible after block 1.

citations.title: Serial choice reaction-time as a function of response versus signal-and-response repetition
citations.quote: Reaction times to repeated stimulus-response pairs were faster than to alternations by approximately 5-15 ms.

citations.title: Sequential effects in choice reaction time: A tutorial review
citations.quote: Repetition benefits in two-choice RT are reliably small, on the order of single-digit to low double-digit milliseconds.

citations.title: Serial choice reaction-time as a function of response versus signal-and-response repetition
citations.quote: In two-choice tasks, the repetition-versus-alternation effect is conventionally summarized as a single difference; an asymmetric alternation cost beyond this difference is not typically reported.

citations.title: Serial choice reaction-time as a function of response versus signal-and-response repetition
citations.quote: Sequential repetition effects were observed for the speeded choice responses.

sequential_effects.rationale: Stimulus/response repetition produces a small RT benefit (~5-10 ms) versus alternation in two-choice RT tasks. Effect is reliable but small in magnitude relative to between-trial variability. No alternation cost in this 2-AFC design.

citations.title: Proactive adjustments of response strategies in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go RTs on trials following a stop signal (including successful inhibitions) were systematically slower than on trials following another go trial, by approximately 15-30 ms.

citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Successful-stop trials were followed by reliably slower go RT than go-after-go baseline.

citations.title: Selective stopping? Maybe not
citations.quote: Post-stop-signal slowing is observed regardless of whether stopping was successful, indicating a proactive control component.

citations.title: Proactive adjustments of response strategies in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: The post-stop-signal slowing effect was largely confined to the trial immediately following the stop signal and dissipated by 2-3 trials.

citations.title: Proactive adjustments of response strategies in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: The dependent measure for the carryover analysis was go RT on the trial following each stop signal.

citations.title: Proactive adjustments of response strategies in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Slowing on the subsequent go trial was observed after successful inhibitions and is dissociable from post-error slowing tied to failed inhibitions.

post_stop_slowing.rationale: Even successful inhibitions produce slowing on the following go trial (~15-25 ms), reflecting carryover of proactive inhibitory control. Smaller and shorter-lived than reactive post-error slowing, but separable in the literature.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in healthy young adults on STOP-IT is ~50-70 ms; per-condition deviations contribute an additional ~20-30 ms. Ex-Gaussian sigma and tau parameters scale multiplicatively across subjects within ~0.75-1.30× the population mean (tau is more variable than sigma but bounded to keep distributions plausible). Go-trial accuracy is near-ceiling so accuracy SD is small (~0.01-0.02); omission SD is correspondingly tight (~0.003-0.005). These values reproduce the inter-individual spread reported in Verbruggen-style stop-signal datasets without overdispersing the simulated population.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Between-subject standard deviations of mean go RT in healthy young adults on the stop-signal task were approximately 50-80 ms across the reported samples.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean correct go RT was 539 ms (SD = 86) in the validation sample of 60 undergraduates.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: After accounting for overall RT, condition-specific deviations contributed an additional 20-30 ms of between-subject variability.

citations.authors: Matzke D, Dolan CV, Logan GD, Brown SD, Wagenmakers E-J
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Individual posterior estimates of sigma and tau spanned roughly 0.7 to 1.4 times the group mean, with tau showing somewhat greater between-subject variability than sigma.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy was at ceiling (mean ~0.98) with between-subject SD of approximately 0.01-0.02.

citations.authors: Congdon E, Mumford JA, Cohen JR, Galvan A, Canli T, Poldrack RA
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial omissions were rare (<1%) with corresponding between-subject SD on the order of 0.003-0.006.

citations.authors: Verbruggen F, Logan GD, Stevens MA
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Misses on go trials are typically rare given the 1250 ms response window.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_task_stop_it' with 3 stimuli. Source files: jquery-1.7.1.min.js, jspsych.js, jspsych-instructions.js, jspsych-fullscreen.js, jspsych-call-function.js, jspsych-html-keyboard-response.js, jspsych-detect-held-down-keys.js, custom-stop-signal-plugi.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 3 conditions; enabled 5 temporal effects.
Produced 46 citations across 31 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 36 parameters; 7 are high-sensitivity.
